text: One of the most dreaded complications of undergoing an esophagectomy is to injure the thoracic duct and have a chylothorax. The most important way to avoid such complication is to confront it and not avoid it like the Voldemort. So to avoid such injuries, we should know the anatomy well of a thoracic duct in the thoracic cage. So let's see the anatomy of thoracic duct. If a patient is lying upright, unlike this case where the patient is in prone position, the anatomy would look something like this, where the number one is the ozygous vein and six is the aorta and between the two, the yellow is the thoracic duct. However, in our case, We perform esophagectomy in a prone position wherein the azagus will be lying up and the aorta behind it and between the two will be the thoracic duct. Remember the heart and the lungs are down and the vertebral body and the vertebra are up at the twelve o'clock position.

In a prone position, the surgeon identifies the mediastinal pleura and the vagus nerve, marked by a red arrow. The dissection proceeds cranially, incising the mediastinal pleura and dissecting outside the vagus nerve, moving towards the azagus vein, marked by a yellow arrow. The azagus vein is held and dissected from all sides, then clipped on both ends towards the superior vena cava and vertebral side, and cut using laparoscopic scissors.

The surgeon clips and cuts the azygos vein using scissors, while noting the thin bronchial artery running behind it at the level of D-IV. A suture is placed across the azygos vein using a Mochi's needle, which pulls the vein upwards to facilitate dissection of its anterior border. Fat is being removed along with the esophagus into the specimen. The intercostal branch from the aorta is identified running behind the thoracic duct, with the red arrow marking the aorta and its adventitia as the dissection reaches the prevascular fascia.

The surgeon uses a red arrow to indicate the intercostal artery branching from the aorta, while dissecting in the plane of the prevascular fascia. The mesoesophagus, consisting of the prevascular fascia and outer mediastinal pleura, is dissected close to the ozygous vein. Fatty tissue and the thoracic duct are included in the specimen. The segmental arteries from the aorta supplying the esophagus are coagulated and cut. The dissection exposes the white adventitia of the aorta, revealing the aortic wall without overlying fatty tissue or fascia, crucial for safe thoracic duct dissection.

The surgeon retracts the esophagus and thoracic duct, revealing the thoracic duct as a wide, glistening transparent tissue between the mediastinal pleura and prevascular fascia. The dissection continues in the prevascular plane, with the fatty tissue beneath the first intercostal artery being removed.

The surgeon incises the outer layer of the mediastinal pleura, revealing the thoracic duct hanging between the two layers of the mesoesophagus. The thoracic duct appears as a white glistening structure between the mediastinal pleura and the prevascular fascia. The esophagus is positioned below, while the aorta is above, covered by a yellowish fat layer. The dissection progresses towards the left mediastinal pleura, with the thoracic duct visibly hanging from above downwards.

The surgeon exposes the mediastinal pleura on the left side to incorporate all fatty tissue into the specimen. The thoracic duct is visible hanging down at the level of the inferior pulmonary vein, where it is safely identified and clipped using metal clips. The aorta is seen dorsally at the twelve o'clock position, with a white sheen on the right side of the thoracic duct and a yellow sheen on the left side, indicating the presence of fat in the first layer of the mesoesophagus.

The important takeaways are that we should always try to identify the thoracic duct, in order to do a radical esophagectomy to have a clear radial margins as well as to avoid the injury to the thoracic duct. Thank you very much.
